title: Cirrhosis — Decompensated
oneLiner: Acute decompensation of cirrhosis with jaundice, ascites, encephalopathy, or variceal bleed – carries ~50% 5-year mortality.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Variceal haemorrhage:** Fresh haematemesis, haemodynamic instability. Single finding: **Tachycardia + haematemesis** – start terlipressin, crossmatch, call OGD.
- **Sepsis (including SBP):** Fever, abdominal pain, confusion, or rising lactate. Single finding: **Abdominal tenderness + rising lactate** – do diagnostic paracentesis and start IV antibiotics.
- **Hepatic encephalopathy Grade 3-4:** GCS drop, asterixis, airway compromise. Single finding: **GCS <13 with no focal neurology** – secure airway, give lactulose enema, check glucose.
- **Hepatorenal syndrome (HRS-AKI):** Rising creatinine >50% from baseline after volume resuscitation. Single finding: **Oliguria with urine Na <10 mmol/L** – stop diuretics, consider terlipressin + albumin.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** New/worsening jaundice, abdominal distension (ascites), confusion/sleep inversion (encephalopathy), haematemesis/melaena (varices), pruritus, fatigue.
- **Key exam findings:** Jaundice, ascites (shifting dullness, fluid thrill), asterixis (hepatic flap), spider angiomata, caput medusae, splenomegaly, peripheral oedema, fetor hepaticus.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely:** Decompensated alcoholic cirrhosis (trigger: infection, variceal bleed, alcohol relapse, medications).
- **Less likely:**
  - Acute alcoholic hepatitis (rapid jaundice, tender hepatomegaly, high bilirubin, no prior cirrhosis – but may overlap).
  - Acute liver failure (no prior liver disease, coagulopathy, encephalopathy within 8 weeks of onset).
  - Cholestatic jaundice (e.g. bile duct obstruction: pain, pale stools, dark urine, dilated ducts on USS).
  - Portal hypertension from non-cirrhotic causes (portal vein thrombosis, schistosomiasis).
- **Splitter:** Tender, palpable liver with fever suggests alcoholic hepatitis; shrunken, nodular liver with ascites favours decompensated cirrhosis.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Capillary glucose (hypoglycaemia mimics encephalopathy), urine dip (infection), pregnancy test (all women childbearing age), NEWS2, bladder scan (retention), **diagnostic paracentesis** – send for neutrophil count, culture, albumin, protein, cytology, Gram stain. **Never delay paracentesis in new ascites or suspected SBP.**
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, INR/PT, albumin, CRP, lactate, ammonia (clinical diagnosis of encephalopathy is better), blood cultures, crossmatch (if variceal bleed), VBG. Consider hepatitis serology, HIV, autoimmune screen, ferritin, caeruloplasmin if aetiology unknown.
- **Imaging:** Urgent liver USS with Doppler (portal vein patency, signs of HCC, ascites). CT abdomen if suspicion of acute abdomen or HCC.
- **Special tests:** OGD (within 24h for variceal bleed, emergency if active bleed). Transjugular liver biopsy if needed.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Child-Pugh score:** Bilirubin, albumin, INR, ascites, encephalopathy – classifies A (well-compensated), B (significant), C (decompensated).
  - **MELD score:** For transplant urgency. MELD >20 = high 3-month mortality.
  - **SBP diagnosis:** Ascitic neutrophil count >250 cells/µL.
- **Never-miss test:** **Ascitic fluid neutrophil count** (to rule in/out SBP). Also, **blood cultures before antibiotics**.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Variceal bleed:** ABC, large-bore IV access, crossmatch, terlipressin (dose per [check local policy]), prophylactic IV antibiotic (e.g. cefotaxime 2g), arrange emergency OGD.
  - **Encephalopathy:** Check glucose, consider naloxone (if opioid use), give lactulose 30-60mL PO/NG or lactulose enema. Avoid benzodiazepines.
  - **SBP:** IV antibiotic (cefotaxime 2g tds or co-amoxiclav 1.2g tds per [local policy]), IV albumin 1.5g/kg on day 1, then 1g/kg on day 3.
  - **HRS-AKI:** Stop all diuretics, hold beta-blockers, give IV albumin 20% (1g/kg), consider terlipressin (dose per [check local policy]) after discussion with hepatology/ICU.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Monitor urine output, daily weight, abdominal girth.
  - Low sodium diet (<2g/day) if ascites/hyponatraemia. Fluid restriction only if Na <125 mmol/L.
  - Lactulose to maintain 2-3 soft stools/day. Consider rifaximin 550mg BD if refractory encephalopathy.
  - Thiamine (Pabrinex) IV BD–TDS for 5 days if alcohol-related.
  - Nutritional support: high protein (1.2-1.5 g/kg) unless refractory encephalopathy.
  - Review/stop beta-blockers (propranolol/carvedilol) during acute decompensation.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Alcohol cessation with addiction service referral.
  - Hepatology follow-up for transplant assessment if MELD >15 or Child-Pugh B/C.
  - Vaccinations: Hep A, Hep B, pneumococcal, influenza, COVID-19.
  - Variceal banding programme for secondary prophylaxis.
  - Lactulose/rifaximin long-term for recurrent encephalopathy.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline cautiously – maintain euvolemia; use 5% dextrose or Hartmann’s if hyponatraemic.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40mg SC daily) unless active variceal bleed or INR >2/platelets <50 – use TED stockings.
  - **Diet:** Low sodium, high protein. NBM if variceal bleed pending OGD.
  - **Observations:** 4-hourly NEWS2, daily weight, strict fluid balance.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Lactulose:** 15-30mL PO BD–QDS, titrate to 2-3 soft stools/d. Enema: 200-300mL PR retained 1 hour.
- **Rifaximin:** 550mg PO BD (for secondary prevention of encephalopathy).
- **Terlipressin:** [check local policy] – typically 0.5-2mg IV

rawMarkdown:
## Cirrhosis — Decompensated
**One-liner:** Acute decompensation of cirrhosis with jaundice, ascites, encephalopathy, or variceal bleed – carries ~50% 5-year mortality.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Variceal haemorrhage:** Fresh haematemesis, haemodynamic instability. Single finding: **Tachycardia + haematemesis** – start terlipressin, crossmatch, call OGD.
- **Sepsis (including SBP):** Fever, abdominal pain, confusion, or rising lactate. Single finding: **Abdominal tenderness + rising lactate** – do diagnostic paracentesis and start IV antibiotics.
- **Hepatic encephalopathy Grade 3-4:** GCS drop, asterixis, airway compromise. Single finding: **GCS <13 with no focal neurology** – secure airway, give lactulose enema, check glucose.
- **Hepatorenal syndrome (HRS-AKI):** Rising creatinine >50% from baseline after volume resuscitation. Single finding: **Oliguria with urine Na <10 mmol/L** – stop diuretics, consider terlipressin + albumin.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** New/worsening jaundice, abdominal distension (ascites), confusion/sleep inversion (encephalopathy), haematemesis/melaena (varices), pruritus, fatigue.
- **Key exam findings:** Jaundice, ascites (shifting dullness, fluid thrill), asterixis (hepatic flap), spider angiomata, caput medusae, splenomegaly, peripheral oedema, fetor hepaticus.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely:** Decompensated alcoholic cirrhosis (trigger: infection, variceal bleed, alcohol relapse, medications).
- **Less likely:**
  - Acute alcoholic hepatitis (rapid jaundice, tender hepatomegaly, high bilirubin, no prior cirrhosis – but may overlap).
  - Acute liver failure (no prior liver disease, coagulopathy, encephalopathy within 8 weeks of onset).
  - Cholestatic jaundice (e.g. bile duct obstruction: pain, pale stools, dark urine, dilated ducts on USS).
  - Portal hypertension from non-cirrhotic causes (portal vein thrombosis, schistosomiasis).
- **Splitter:** Tender, palpable liver with fever suggests alcoholic hepatitis; shrunken, nodular liver with ascites favours decompensated cirrhosis.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Capillary glucose (hypoglycaemia mimics encephalopathy), urine dip (infection), pregnancy test (all women childbearing age), NEWS2, bladder scan (retention), **diagnostic paracentesis** – send for neutrophil count, culture, albumin, protein, cytology, Gram stain. **Never delay paracentesis in new ascites or suspected SBP.**
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, INR/PT, albumin, CRP, lactate, ammonia (clinical diagnosis of encephalopathy is better), blood cultures, crossmatch (if variceal bleed), VBG. Consider hepatitis serology, HIV, autoimmune screen, ferritin, caeruloplasmin if aetiology unknown.
- **Imaging:** Urgent liver USS with Doppler (portal vein patency, signs of HCC, ascites). CT abdomen if suspicion of acute abdomen or HCC.
- **Special tests:** OGD (within 24h for variceal bleed, emergency if active bleed). Transjugular liver biopsy if needed.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Child-Pugh score:** Bilirubin, albumin, INR, ascites, encephalopathy – classifies A (well-compensated), B (significant), C (decompensated).
  - **MELD score:** For transplant urgency. MELD >20 = high 3-month mortality.
  - **SBP diagnosis:** Ascitic neutrophil count >250 cells/µL.
- **Never-miss test:** **Ascitic fluid neutrophil count** (to rule in/out SBP). Also, **blood cultures before antibiotics**.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Variceal bleed:** ABC, large-bore IV access, crossmatch, terlipressin (dose per [check local policy]), prophylactic IV antibiotic (e.g. cefotaxime 2g), arrange emergency OGD.
  - **Encephalopathy:** Check glucose, consider naloxone (if opioid use), give lactulose 30-60mL PO/NG or lactulose enema. Avoid benzodiazepines.
  - **SBP:** IV antibiotic (cefotaxime 2g tds or co-amoxiclav 1.2g tds per [local policy]), IV albumin 1.5g/kg on day 1, then 1g/kg on day 3.
  - **HRS-AKI:** Stop all diuretics, hold beta-blockers, give IV albumin 20% (1g/kg), consider terlipressin (dose per [check local policy]) after discussion with hepatology/ICU.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Monitor urine output, daily weight, abdominal girth.
  - Low sodium diet (<2g/day) if ascites/hyponatraemia. Fluid restriction only if Na <125 mmol/L.
  - Lactulose to maintain 2-3 soft stools/day. Consider rifaximin 550mg BD if refractory encephalopathy.
  - Thiamine (Pabrinex) IV BD–TDS for 5 days if alcohol-related.
  - Nutritional support: high protein (1.2-1.5 g/kg) unless refractory encephalopathy.
  - Review/stop beta-blockers (propranolol/carvedilol) during acute decompensation.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Alcohol cessation with addiction service referral.
  - Hepatology follow-up for transplant assessment if MELD >15 or Child-Pugh B/C.
  - Vaccinations: Hep A, Hep B, pneumococcal, influenza, COVID-19.
  - Variceal banding programme for secondary prophylaxis.
  - Lactulose/rifaximin long-term for recurrent encephalopathy.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline cautiously – maintain euvolemia; use 5% dextrose or Hartmann’s if hyponatraemic.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40mg SC daily) unless active variceal bleed or INR >2/platelets <50 – use TED stockings.
  - **Diet:** Low sodium, high protein. NBM if variceal bleed pending OGD.
  - **Observations:** 4-hourly NEWS2, daily weight, strict fluid balance.

### Drugs & Doses
- **Lactulose:** 15-30mL PO BD–QDS, titrate to 2-3 soft stools/d. Enema: 200-300mL PR retained 1 hour.
- **Rifaximin:** 550mg PO BD (for secondary prevention of encephalopathy).
- **Terlipressin:** [check local policy] – typically 0.5-2mg IV
